narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting the likelihood of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.70, LSAT score of 32, full-time student status, and your Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings of 1—and estimated a 65.9% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This high risk of failure is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students predicted to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 academic decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom 10% of your school that year—far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing—suggesting challenges in sustaining strong performance as coursework intensified. Your Year 1 decile ranking in the bottom 10% also contributed to this risk, reinforcing a pattern of early struggles compared to peers who start stronger on average. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA played a helpful role, pulling the prediction somewhat toward success since your 2.70, while below the average of 3.21, still indicated some foundational capability. Overall, these academic rankings outweighed other elements like your LSAT or family income, which had less sway in this decision, especially when stacked against students who typically rank higher in school deciles and show steadier progress. Your full-time status aligned with the vast majority of applicants, so it didn't stand out either way. This outcome reflects how the model weighs consistent academic momentum as a key bar exam predictor for applicants like you. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score above the average 36.7, or strengthening your recent grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program to improve future decile rankings. Building a clearer track record of academic resilience could shift your predicted odds dramatically for the next application cycle. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by targeting these areas, and with focused effort, you can too.
